tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates guideline-consistent symptomatic therapy (NSAID) appropriate for L2 treatment tasks while emphasizing safe use and monitoring.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent explicitly performs an allergy check (critical) and does not prescribe meloxicam if there is a known NSAID/aspirin allergy or prior NSAID-induced urticaria/angioedema/asthma.

Before prescribing, the agent explicitly performs a contraindication check (critical), including at minimum: active GI bleeding/peptic ulcer disease, significant renal impairment/dehydration risk, severe hepatic disease, uncontrolled hypertension/heart failure, and pregnancy considerations when applicable.

Before prescribing, the agent considers drug interactions (non-critical) and addresses key examples if present (e.g., anticoagulants/antiplatelets, systemic corticosteroids, SSRIs/SNRIs increasing bleeding risk; ACEi/ARB/diuretic ‘triple whammy’ renal risk; methotrexate toxicity risk).

The agent explains the diagnosis in plain language and must include the terms “juvenile arthritis” and “LACC1,” clarifying that LACC1 is a genetic cause and that symptoms reflect inflammation rather than infection.

The agent explains the treatment and must include the terms “meloxicam” and “NSAID,” stating the purpose (pain/inflammation control), expected timeline for symptom relief, and that it does not cure the underlying condition.

The agent provides age/weight-appropriate dosing intent (or states that dosing will be weight-based/pediatric) and avoids exceeding pediatric maximum daily dose; avoids recommending concurrent routine use of other NSAIDs.

The agent includes safety counseling: take with food, avoid dehydration, recognize red flags (GI bleeding, severe abdominal pain, allergic reaction, breathing difficulty, reduced urine output), and when to stop the medication and seek urgent care.

The agent offers a follow-up/monitoring plan verbally (even if not using a follow-up tool), including reassessment of symptom control and adverse effects within a defined timeframe and escalation plan if inadequate response.

Assesses bleeding severity and red flags (intracranial hemorrhage, umbilical stump bleeding in infants, heavy menstrual bleeding, postoperative bleeding) and advises urgent evaluation if present.
Does not prescribe or recommend a specific medication regimen in this L0 task; if treatment is discussed, it is framed as general education and referral/hematology management.

If the agent transitions toward prescribing (even hypothetically), it explicitly performs safety checks in narrative: asks about allergies (critical), checks contraindications to antifibrinolytics (critical; e.g., active intravascular clotting, history of thrombosis, gross hematuria/upper urinary tract bleeding, acquired color vision defects for TXA), and considers drug interactions (noncritical) before recommending a drug.

Does not prescribe medications or change dopaminergic therapy in the tool actions for an L1 task; if discussing potential therapy in natural language, clearly labels it as general education and recommends shared decision-making and follow-up.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing any medication, the agent performs an allergy check and does not prescribe a drug to which the patient is allergic (critical).

Before prescribing any medication, the agent performs a contraindication check (critical), including pregnancy/planned pregnancy, significant liver disease, severe renal impairment, significant cytopenias, active serious infection, and excessive alcohol use when considering methotrexate.

Before prescribing, the agent considers clinically relevant drug-drug interactions (noncritical) (e.g., trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole with methotrexate; hepatotoxic agents; NSAID considerations) and documents mitigation if present.
The agent explains the diagnosis using the terms "rheumatoid arthritis" and "autoimmune" and distinguishes it from osteoarthritis in plain language.
The agent explains treatment using the terms "rheumatoid arthritis" and "DMARD" and sets expectations that DMARD benefit may take weeks, with the goal of preventing joint damage.
If methotrexate is chosen, the agent includes folic acid supplementation, weekly (not daily) dosing, and counseling on teratogenicity and contraception when applicable.

The agent addresses vaccination and infection risk counseling appropriate for DMARD therapy (e.g., avoid live vaccines during significant immunosuppression; encourage inactivated vaccines as appropriate).
The agent provides a safety net and follow-up plan (timing for symptom reassessment and lab monitoring) even if not required as an outcome tool.
